FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Periods 2-3 follow-up (2018-2021), 9095 ABCD adolescents ages 10-13

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective linear regression models estimating associations between cyberbullying victimization and manic symptoms

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying alone fails IC2 per binding clarification; exposure is cyberbullying victimization (lifetime, past 12-month) not a qualifying phone/SM variable; no separate phone/SM variable with own coefficient reported

notes: Paper focuses on cyberbullying (online harm) as IV; cyberbullying does not qualify as a phone/SM variable per v7 binding rules

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 3 (2019-2021), N=10,054, ages 12-13 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: ordinal logistic regression models assessing associations among media parenting practices and consumption of R-rated movies / mature-rated video games

FT_IC2.evidence: R-rated content and mature-rated gaming alone fail IC2 per binding clarifications; no separate phone/SM variable with own coefficient reported; media parenting practices are covariates/predictors, not qualifying SM/phone exposures

notes: Content rating (R-rated movies, mature-rated games) alone does not qualify; parenting practices predict mature content consumption, not SM/phone use

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, longitudinal design, baseline 2016-2018 (ages 9-10) through Year 3 2019-2021 (ages 12-13), N=9,519

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal mixed-effects modeling with compositional data analysis (CoDA) over four annual assessments

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 identifies phone/SM-specific estimates (video games, digital socializing, TV/movies, total screen time; Table 2); however, other-side outcomes are time-use composition variables (sleep duration, sports/exercise activity, other activities) not individual-trait-level constructs; analysis examines displacement of activities within 24-hour composition, not associations between SM/phone use and neural, behavioral, health, or psychosocial traits

notes: Screen time modalities qualify (video games, digital socializing, TV) on Step 1; other-side fails: sleep and sports/exercise are time-allocation measures, not individual-trait outcomes
